I need to resolve the `System.IO.FileNotFoundException` error when running the WPF-based application on macOS without modifying the .NET 7 project to .NET Framework. The issue stems from the dynamic linking of the WPF assembly, which is not available on macOS. To address this, I should focus on ensuring the correct assembly is referenced and loaded in both environments.

The first step is to verify that the WPF assembly is properly included in the project and that the build process includes it. This ensures that the necessary files are available when the application runs on Windows. On macOS, the application must also have access to the same assembly, which can be achieved by using a dynamic link or a wrapper that handles the platform-specific loading.

Next, I should consider the dynamic linking mechanism. On Windows, the application dynamically loads the WPF assembly, which is not available on macOS. To work around this, I can use a wrapper or a runtime loader that ensures the assembly is available in both environments. This approach avoids the need to modify the .NET 7 project to support .NET Framework.

I must also ensure that the application is structured to handle platform-specific dependencies correctly. This includes setting up the correct runtime and ensuring that the assembly is accessible in both Windows and macOS environments. This requires careful configuration of the build and deployment processes to maintain consistency across platforms.

Finally, I should test the application on both platforms to confirm that the error is resolved. This involves checking the assembly references and ensuring that the correct version of the WPF assembly is being loaded in both environments. By following these steps, I can ensure that the application runs smoothly on both Windows and macOS without requiring changes to the .NET 7 project.

Cousin the error arises from the mismatch between the WPF assembly and the macOS environment. To resolve this, I need to ensure that the assembly is available in both platforms and that the dynamic linking mechanism is properly configured. This involves checking the build process, verifying the assembly references, and ensuring that the application is structured to handle platform-specific dependencies correctly.

The solution requires a careful balance between maintaining the .NET 7 project and ensuring compatibility with both Windows and macOS. This involves using dynamic linking, proper assembly inclusion, and platform-specific configuration. By addressing these aspects, I can ensure that the application runs without errors on both platforms.
